title: Literature Review
text:
A major change in perspective is taking place in the field of credit risk management, by adding a dynamic Machine Learning (ML) frameworks that prioritize regulatory interpretability and prediction accuracy replacing static statistical scoring system.
In the past, logistic modeling and other basic linear models were used for evaluating risk from credit score or assessment . However, Bussmann et al. (2020)[1] suggested that modern artificial intelligence models greatly improved the ability to recognize complicated, dynamic trends in financial organizations. Bussmann et al. (2020)[1] highlighted that these models need to be explained so as to be actually used in handling risks. They demonstrate how model outcomes can be split down into specific feature components by using statistical techniques like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), enabling management to validate the theories and at the same time derive the choices regarding credit. Shreya and Pathak (2025)[2] support this by emphasizing that XAI is the main link between the efficiency of ML and the integrity standards of modern banking institutions.
The use of XAI is a solution to worldwide regulations rather than this current technological solution. In their research on the use of XAI in banks, De Lange et al. (2022)[3] pointed out that it is technically challenging to utilize transparent modelling for credit rejections due to the “obligation to explanation” required by standards like the General Data Protection Regulation as per the the laws. According to their study, while collaborative methods like Gradient Boosting deliver high AUC ratings, they need specific explanatory tools (like Waterfall , Agile or Impact charts) trying to provide consumers and the banking industry the fine-grained insights they need for credit score processing
A diverse approach in the modern world that integrates quantitative data science with business goals and strategy is increasingly being used to analyze current consumer’s evaluations of credit. Oyeyemi et al. (2025)[4] support a comprehensive approach in which XAI is a fundamental part of organizational analytics instead of only a post-hoc analysis method. They claim that institutions may more focus accurately correlate their risk profile with economic circumstances by explaining the “why” underlying model results, turning a technical forecast into a useful business insight for the advancement.
According to current business viewpoints, autonomous technologies will replace the human interpretation in the next generation of credit risk and any other areas as well. The projected growth of corporate credit, according to McKinsey & Company (2025)[5], is “agentic,” in which AI agents control the entire credit procedure—from initial data collection to final decision-making after going through the process—all while enforcing an extensive level of accessibility for consumer’s trust.
To go further, Kubam (2024)[6] outlines an algorithm of “Agentic AI” that permits independent, quick, and understandable making of decisions. As a way to maintain manual human-oriented control over the machine even as it grows increasingly autonomous, these agents are built to track their own outcomes and provide simple analyses of their decision making. This project understands this dynamic horizon as the setting in which powerful, transparent models will ultimately work, even if it concentrates on using LightGBM as the primary estimation algorithm.
Based on the reviewed literature, I think there is still a critical need for frameworks that can handle the particular characteristics of banking information (such as disparities in classes and classification characteristics) without losing accessibility towards customers, even though the financial services sector is quickly trying to move to complicated, high-speed algorithms like LightGBM. As developing a LightGBM pipeline that makes use of Shapley values \((\phi_i)\) to offer a strong auditing trail for credit, this project covers that gap and assures that the algorithm is both “state-of-the-art” in terms efficiency and “state-of-the-compliant” in terms of accessibility.

title: Literature Review
text:
A major change in perspective is taking place in the field of credit risk management, by adding a dynamic Machine Learning (ML) frameworks that prioritize regulatory interpretability and prediction accuracy replacing static statistical scoring system.
In the past, logistic modeling and other basic linear models were used for evaluating risk from credit score or assessment . However, Bussmann et al. (2020)[1] suggested that modern artificial intelligence models greatly improved the ability to recognize complicated, dynamic trends in financial organizations. Bussmann et al. (2020)[1] highlighted that these models need to be explained so as to be actually used in handling risks. They demonstrate how model outcomes can be split down into specific feature components by using statistical techniques like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), enabling management to validate the theories and at the same time derive the choices regarding credit. Shreya and Pathak (2025)[2] support this by emphasizing that XAI is the main link between the efficiency of ML and the integrity standards of modern banking institutions.
The use of XAI is a solution to worldwide regulations rather than this current technological solution. In their research on the use of XAI in banks, De Lange et al. (2022)[3] pointed out that it is technically challenging to utilize transparent modelling for credit rejections due to the “obligation to explanation” required by standards like the General Data Protection Regulation as per the the laws. According to their study, while collaborative methods like Gradient Boosting deliver high AUC ratings, they need specific explanatory tools (like Waterfall , Agile or Impact charts) trying to provide consumers and the banking industry the fine-grained insights they need for credit score processing
A diverse approach in the modern world that integrates quantitative data science with business goals and strategy is increasingly being used to analyze current consumer’s evaluations of credit. Oyeyemi et al. (2025)[4] support a comprehensive approach in which XAI is a fundamental part of organizational analytics instead of only a post-hoc analysis method. They claim that institutions may more focus accurately correlate their risk profile with economic circumstances by explaining the “why” underlying model results, turning a technical forecast into a useful business insight for the advancement.
According to current business viewpoints, autonomous technologies will replace the human interpretation in the next generation of credit risk and any other areas as well. The projected growth of corporate credit, according to McKinsey & Company (2025)[5], is “agentic,” in which AI agents control the entire credit procedure—from initial data collection to final decision-making after going through the process—all while enforcing an extensive level of accessibility for consumer’s trust.
To go further, Kubam (2024)[6] outlines an algorithm of “Agentic AI” that permits independent, quick, and understandable making of decisions. As a way to maintain manual human-oriented control over the machine even as it grows increasingly autonomous, these agents are built to track their own outcomes and provide simple analyses of their decision making. This project understands this dynamic horizon as the setting in which powerful, transparent models will ultimately work, even if it concentrates on using LightGBM as the primary estimation algorithm.
Based on the reviewed literature, I think there is still a critical need for frameworks that can handle the particular characteristics of banking information (such as disparities in classes and classification characteristics) without losing accessibility towards customers, even though the financial services sector is quickly trying to move to complicated, high-speed algorithms like LightGBM. As developing a LightGBM pipeline that makes use of Shapley values \((\phi_i)\) to offer a strong auditing trail for credit, this project covers that gap and assures that the algorithm is both “state-of-the-art” in terms efficiency and “state-of-the-compliant” in terms of accessibility.

title: Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
Evaluation of credit risk is vital for the financial services industry because, it is required to avoid risks and maintain compliance with regulations, organizations have to accurately predict a borrower’s likelihood of failure while re-payment. Because they are straightforward and easy to understand for anyone, typical evaluation methods like systems based on rules and regression analysis have been often used in AI world. Modern advancement in machine learning, although have shown that the hierarchical (structural) financial data can now be predicted with significant advantages.
The Risk of default Assessment, which measures a lender’s probability count of defaulting lenders, it is an important aspect of organizational stability in the technological current days in financial environment. The industry has already started moving from restrictive rule-wise structures to complicated Machine Learning (ML) architectures, as stated by Shreya and Pathak (2025)[1]. Although the fact is these models provide past unheard-of precision in forecasting, they are frequently addressed to as “black boxes.” The paper uses Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to overcome the visibility gap and LightGBM to develop a robust credit assessment engine.
According to author de Lange et al. (2022)[2] and Bussmann et al. (2020)[3], financial businesses should offer a “Right to justification” as a way to make sure that risk management is the responsibility for both moral and verifiable. This flexibility is not just a technical preference in organization the but also a legal need. In a similar vein, de Lange et al. (2022)[2] demonstrate that explainable AI methods can maintain accurate predictions while working on to improve an accessibility in financial systems nowadays. The significance of combining the both concepts explainable machine learning with business analytics for ethical evaluation of credit risk defaulters have been demonstrated by recent studies by Shreya and Pathak (2025)[1] and Oyeyemi et al. (2025)[4].
Here are the important steps to develop a machine learning-based credit risk model to build overall good performance:

The first is collecting and preparing the data, including the encrypting variables with categories and finding the missing values.
Dividing the dataset into two subsets for testing and training.
Applying the training data to train for the LightGBM classifier.
Assessing predictive performance for the the model with metrics like ROC-AUC, accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
Utilizing SHAP-based explanation and characteristic significance for analyzing the model forecasts.

This method provides that the model is easy to understand, versatile, and trustworthy. The predictive model’s accuracy on unidentified information is determined using model evaluation approaches including cross-validity and train-test partitioning.
This project is using the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) programmed in Python langauge to create an Explainable Credit Risk Assessment system, driven by major breakthroughs in both research and business. LightGBM is determined because it performs well on structured table type datasets and is effective and versatile way. The Credit Risk Dataset’s consumer demographic details, banking, and credit historical information will be used by the algorithm to forecast defaults listing on loans potential. Explainable methods like SHAP will be used to understand both specific and worldwide projections in order to assure integrity and transperency.
The objective of this study is to show how explainable artificial intelligence concepts may be integrated with efficient boosting algorithms to create precise, accessible, and insightful credit risk evaluation solutions to avoid the losses of financial organization.

Assessing predictive performance for the the model with metrics like ROC-AUC, accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

title: Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
2. Methods

2.1 Data Preprocessing
First, preprocessing is for the dataset that includes borrower demographic details, financial level, and credit history characteristics. To utilize statistical robustness and lessen vulnerability to extreme values, missing numerical data are handled via median imputation. One-hot encoding is used to convert categorical variables into numerical representation so that gradient boosting calculation can use them for prediction.


2.2 LightGBM Architecture for Credit Risk Prediction
This reasearch uses Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) to predict the possibility of loan defaulter’s based on borrower charactestics like, how much they are earning, employment history, how much load amount and history of credit. LightGBM is an really efficient as a boosting framework to make decision making tress sequentially to get accurate predictiona and reduce the errors in the prediction.
The above function is optimized by the LightGBM model:
\[
\text{Obj}(t)=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
l\left(
y_i,
\hat{y}_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)
\right)
+\Omega(f_t)
\]
𝑦𝑖 indicates if the loan is in default or not. (default or non-default)
𝑦^i(t−1) is the value expected from the earlier loop.
𝑓𝑡(𝑥𝑖) shows the new decision tree that was added after iteration
l(⋅) represent the binary cross-entropy value for classifier loss function.
Ω(ft) is the normalization idea that keeps excessive fitting from developing.


2.3 LightGBM Model Implementation


2.4 Explainable Agentic AI Framework
An explainable agentic reasoning loop develop on the observe-reason-act model has been integrated with the system. The agent develops understandable explanations which is human-readable for credit risk evaluation through evaluating significant variables and observing predictive model outcomes through system.
The cycle of agentic reasoning is stated as:
Observation → Reasoning→ Decision → Explanation
To provide relevant risk assessment, the framework analyzes borrower some characteristics such as financial status, credit amount, background in employment, and credit history.


2.5 Model Training and Evaluation
This study spliting the dataset into training and testing subsets using stratified sampling and it keeps the balance of class classification. Gradient boosting iterations with optimized hyperparameters, such as learning rate and number of leaves, are used to train the model.
To guarantee dependable classification performance, model performance is assessed using ROC-AUC score, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix analysis.

title: Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
3. Analysis and Results

3.1 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) :

3.1.1 Data Description :
The present research is using the Credit Risk Dataset, which contains 32,581 borrower records and twelve variables related to financial, credit history, and demographic details. A borrower’s likelihood of defaulting (1) or not (0) is indicated by the binary target variable loan_status. The dataset includes both quantitative and qualitative factors, including income, loan amount, interest rate, length of work, home ownership status, and loan purpose. It is perfect for applying LightGBM and evaluating explainable, agentic AI decision-making in credit risk prediction due to its realistic structure and suitable size.

3.1.2 Data Ingestion (Python) :
The pandas package in Python was used to load the dataset. A dataframe called credit1 was created by reading the file credit_risk_dataset.csv. The dataset was first explored by examining column data types, previewing the first few records, and determining its dimensions. To guarantee effective ingestion and comprehend variable composition, the dataset structure was validated using the.shape(),.head(), and.info() functions.


Code
import os
import pandas as pd
from skimpy import skim

def find_git_root(start=None):
    if start is None:
        start = os.getcwd()
    path = os.path.abspath(start)
    
    while True:
        if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(path, ".git")):
            return path
        
        parent = os.path.dirname(path)
        if parent == path:
            raise FileNotFoundError("No .git directory found — are you inside a Git repository?")
        
        path = parent

repo_root = find_git_root()
datasets_path = os.path.join(repo_root, "datasets")

# Reading the datafile credit_risk_dataset.csv
credit_path = os.path.join(datasets_path, "credit_risk_dataset.csv")
credit1 = pd.read_csv(credit_path)

u### 3.1. Data Ingestion
This code chunk is importing the dataset from the folder datasets which it is finding at the root of the github repo and it is concatenating the path to run the function read_csv from tidyverse.


Code
find_git_root <- function(start = getwd()) {
  path <- normalizePath(start, winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)
  while (path != dirname(path)) {
    if (dir.exists(file.path(path, ".git"))) return(path)
    path <- dirname(path)
  }
  stop("No .git directory found — are you inside a Git repository?")
}

repo_root <- find_git_root()
datasets_path <- file.path(repo_root, "datasets")

# Reading the datafile credit_risk_dataset.csv
credit_path <- file.path(datasets_path, "credit_risk_dataset.csv")
credit1 = read_csv(credit_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

Code
import os
import pandas as pd

def find_git_root(start=None):
    if start is None:
        start = os.getcwd()
    path = os.path.abspath(start)
    
    while True:
        if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(path, ".git")):
            return path
        
        parent = os.path.dirname(path)
        if parent == path:
            raise FileNotFoundError("No .git directory found — are you inside a Git repository?")
        
        path = parent

repo_root = find_git_root()
datasets_path = os.path.join(repo_root, "datasets")

# Reading the datafile credit_risk_dataset.csv
credit_path = os.path.join(datasets_path, "credit_risk_dataset.csv")
credit1 = pd.read_csv(credit_path)



from skimpy import skim

skim(credit1)

title: Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text: 4. Conclusion

title: Explainable Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
A vital role for financial institutions is evaluating credit risk, which has a direct impact on lending choices, portfolio performance, and regulatory compliance. Because they are straightforward and easy to understand, traditional credit scoring techniques like logistic regression have gained widespread use. These conventional models, however, find it difficult to account for nonlinear patterns and complicated borrower behaviors as financial data grows in size, complexity, and heterogeneity.
The predictive accuracy of credit risk modeling has greatly increased as a result of recent developments in machine learning, especially ensemble-based techniques like Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT). Among these techniques, the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) has shown itself to be a very effective and scalable framework that can deal with imbalanced and high-dimensional credit datasets. Although LightGBM performs well, it is frequently perceived as a “black-box” paradigm, which restricts transparency and undermines confidence among auditors, regulators, and business users.
This paper suggests an Explainable Agentic AI framework for credit risk assessment utilizing LightGBM in order to overcome these issues. The framework combines an intelligent agentic decision layer that analyzes model predictions, produces insightful explanations, and suggests suitable courses of action with SHAP-based explainability. The suggested approach seeks to facilitate responsible and explicable credit decision-making in regulated financial environments by fusing prediction accuracy, transparency, and adaptive reasoning.

These conventional models, however, find it difficult to account for nonlinear patterns and complicated borrower behaviors as financial data grows in size, complexity, and heterogeneity.
The predictive accuracy of credit risk modeling has greatly increased as a result of recent developments in machine learning, especially ensemble-based techniques like Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT). Among these techniques, the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) has shown itself to be a very effective and scalable framework that can deal with imbalanced and high-dimensional credit datasets. Although LightGBM performs well, it is frequently perceived as a “black-box” paradigm, which restricts transparency and undermines confidence among auditors, regulators, and business users.
This paper suggests an Explainable Agentic AI framework for credit risk assessment utilizing LightGBM in order to overcome these issues. The framework combines an intelligent agentic decision layer that analyzes model predictions, produces insightful explanations, and suggests suitable courses of action with SHAP-based explainability. The suggested approach seeks to facilitate responsible and explicable credit decision-making in regulated financial environments by fusing prediction accuracy, transparency, and adaptive reasoning.

title: Present a great story for data science projects
text:
Introduction
In financial institutions, evaluating credit risk is essential since it has a direct bearing on lending choices, portfolio performance, and regulatory compliance. The simplicity and interpretability of traditional credit scoring models, including logistic regression, have led to their widespread use. Unfortunately, these models are less successful due to the increasing number and complexity of financial data, as they are unable to capture complicated borrower behavior and non-linear correlations. Credit risk prediction has been greatly enhanced by recent developments in machine learning, particularly ensemble techniques like Gradient Boosting Decision Trees. Large, high-dimensional, and unbalanced credit datasets can be handled using the scalable and potent Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) model. LightGBM is frequently regarded as a black-box approach, which restricts transparency and erodes confidence among regulators and business users despite its impressive performance. This study suggests an Explainable Agentic AI framework for credit risk assessment utilizing LightGBM in order to overcome these issues. An intelligent agentic decision layer that can read predictions, produce explanations, and suggest actions is combined with LightGBM and SHAP-based explainability. The system seeks to promote responsible and explicable credit judgments in regulated financial contexts by combining accuracy, transparency, and adaptive decision-making.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.arange(0, 2, 0.01)
theta = 2 * np.pi * r
fig, ax = plt.subplots(
  subplot_kw = {'projection': 'polar'} 
)
ax.plot(theta, r)
ax.set_rticks([0.5, 1, 1.5, 2])
ax.grid(True)
plt.show()

Code
import os
import pandas as pd

def find_git_root(start=None):
    if start is None:
        start = os.getcwd()
    path = os.path.abspath(start)
    
    while True:
        if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(path, ".git")):
            return path
        
        parent = os.path.dirname(path)
        if parent == path:
            raise FileNotFoundError("No .git directory found — are you inside a Git repository?")
        
        path = parent

repo_root = find_git_root()
datasets_path = os.path.join(repo_root, "datasets")

# Reading the datafile credit_risk_dataset.csv
credit_path = os.path.join(datasets_path, "credit_risk_dataset.csv")
credit1 = pd.read_csv(credit_path)

title: Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
5. References


1. Shreya, & Pathak, H. (2025). Explainable artificial intelligence credit risk assessment using machine learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.19383


2. De Lange, P. E., Melsom, B., Vennerød, C. B., & Westgaard, S. (2022). Explainable AI for credit assessment in banks. Journal of Risk and Financial Management, 15(12), 556.


3. Bussmann, N., Giudici, P., Marinelli, D., & Papenbrock, J. (2021). Explainable machine learning in credit risk management. Computational Economics, 57(1), 203–216.


4. Oyeyemi, D. O., Okosieme, O. O., Idowu-Kunlere, O., Julius, E. A., & Nwinyi, I. P. (n.d.). Explainable AI for credit risk assessment: Integrating machine learning with business analytics.

title: Explainable Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
LightGBM Ensemble Learning Objective
The LightGBM model optimizes a regularized loss function given by:
[ ()= _{i=1}^{N} l(y_i,i) + {k=1}^{T} (f_k) ]
where the loss function and regularization terms are defined as above.
where:
𝑦𝑖 indicates if the loan is in default or not. (default or non-default)
𝑦^i(t−1) is the value expected from the earlier loop.
𝑓𝑡(𝑥𝑖) shows the new decision tree that was added after iteration
l(⋅) represent the binary cross-entropy value for classifier loss function.
Ω(ft) is the normalization idea that keeps excessive fitting from developing.

2.3 LightGBM Model Implementation


2.4 Explainable Agentic AI Framework
An explainable agentic reasoning loop develop on the observe-reason-act model has been integrated with the system. The agent develops understandable explanations which is human-readable for credit risk evaluation through evaluating significant variables and observing predictive model outcomes through system.
The cycle of agentic reasoning is stated as:
Observation → Reasoning→ Decision → Explanation
To provide relevant risk assessment, the framework analyzes borrower some characteristics such as financial status, credit amount, background in employment, and credit history.


2.5 Model Training and Evaluation
This study spliting the dataset into training and testing subsets using stratified sampling and it keeps the balance of class classification. Gradient boosting iterations with optimized hyperparameters, such as learning rate and number of leaves, are used to train the model.
To guarantee dependable classification performance, model performance is assessed using ROC-AUC score, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix analysis.
